description: Common Nepali greetings, phrases, and expressions for TTS text normalization and corpus generation

notes.usage: Reference data for generating natural Nepali TTS training sentences covering greetings, farewells, thanks, apologies, wishes, condolences, polite expressions, conversational phrases, and festival greetings
notes.formality_levels: Nepali has distinct formal (tapaaĩ/hajur), neutral, and informal (timi/ta) registers; formality is marked per entry
notes.honorifics: Nepali verbs conjugate differently based on respect level; phrases here use the appropriate verb forms for their marked formality
notes.context: Many phrases serve dual purposes (e.g., namaste for both greeting and farewell); usage_context clarifies primary use

greetings.usage_context: Universal greeting used at any time of day; appropriate for all social contexts
greetings.description: The most common Nepali greeting, used with palms joined (anjali mudra)

greetings.usage_context: Formal greeting used in official settings, speeches, or with elders
greetings.description: More formal variant of namaste; often used in written or broadcast Nepali

greetings.usage_context: Used to greet elders, teachers, or revered persons with deep respect
greetings.description: Highly respectful greeting typically accompanied by bowing or touching feet

greetings.usage_context: Morning greeting, commonly used in media, formal settings, and educated speech
greetings.description: Sanskrit-derived morning greeting; more common in formal/literary Nepali than everyday speech

greetings.usage_context: Daytime greeting used in formal or literary contexts
greetings.description: Less commonly used in daily conversation; more frequent in written and broadcast Nepali

greetings.usage_context: Evening greeting used in formal settings and media
greetings.description: Sanskrit-derived evening greeting; used in news broadcasts and formal events

greetings.usage_context: Nighttime farewell or greeting; used before sleeping or parting at night
greetings.description: Formal good-night expression; colloquially people often just say namaste

greetings.usage_context: Polite inquiry about well-being when greeting someone respectfully
greetings.description: Formal second-person form using hunuhunchha (high honorific verb)

farewells.usage_context: Used as farewell in addition to greeting; the most common way to say goodbye
farewells.description: Same word used for both hello and goodbye in Nepali

farewells.usage_context: Formal farewell, often used when someone is leaving for a long time
farewells.description: Literary/formal farewell term; also means 'leave' or 'holiday'

farewells.usage_context: Hopeful farewell expressing expectation of meeting again
farewells.description: Common parting phrase conveying hope for future reunion

farewells.usage_context: Used when seeing someone off; often followed by well-wishes
farewells.description: Transitional phrase used at the moment of departure

farewells.usage_context: Said to someone embarking on a trip or journey
farewells.description: Formal travel blessing combining shubha (auspicious) and yaatraa (journey)

farewells.usage_context: Polite farewell to a guest or visitor, inviting them to return
farewells.description: Hospitable parting phrase using honorific verb form

farewells.usage_context: Caring farewell wishing someone to look after themselves
farewells.description: Formal expression of concern at parting
Common way to signal leaving; often said before the actual farewell

thanks_and_gratitude.usage_context: Standard expression of gratitude in formal and semi-formal contexts
thanks_and_gratitude.description: The most common formal thank-you; Sanskrit-derived

thanks_and_gratitude.usage_context: Emphatic gratitude for significant help or kindness
Deep gratitude in formal speech, writing, or official acknowledgments
thanks_and_gratitude.description: Highly formal expression of gratitude; literary register

thanks_and_gratitude.english: I am happy / I am pleased
thanks_and_gratitude.usage_context: Expressing pleasure or gratitude upon receiving help or good news
Attributing a positive outcome to someone's generosity or grace
thanks_and_gratitude.description: Humble expression of gratitude acknowledging the other person's role

thanks_and_gratitude.usage_context: Very emphatic gratitude, often for extraordinary help
thanks_and_gratitude.description: Reduplicated intensifier for maximum emphasis on gratitude

thanks_and_gratitude.usage_context: Thanking someone for their help or collaboration in a task
thanks_and_gratitude.description: Commonly used in professional and organizational contexts

thanks_and_gratitude.usage_context: Acknowledging a significant favor or act of kindness
thanks_and_gratitude.description: Expresses deep obligation; often used when someone has gone out of their way to help

apologies.usage_context: Standard polite apology or request for pardon; also used to get attention
apologies.description: Most common formal apology using honorific verb form

apologies.usage_context: Highly formal apology used in official or literary contexts
apologies.description: Sanskrit-derived formal apology; more literary than maaf garnuhos

apologies.usage_context: Expressing regret or sorrow about a situation
apologies.description: Conveys personal sadness or regret; used both as apology and expression of sympathy

apologies.usage_context: Earnest request for forgiveness for a specific wrong
apologies.description: More personal and direct appeal for forgiveness

apologies.english: A mistake happened / I made a mistake
apologies.description: Neutral admission of error; the passive construction is common in Nepali apologies
More direct and personal than galti bhayo; takes explicit responsibility

apologies.usage_context: Formal request for pardon in serious or official situations
Apologizing specifically for being late or a delay in response
apologies.description: Common in professional and official communication

congratulations_and_wishes.usage_context: General congratulations for achievements, milestones, or happy events
congratulations_and_wishes.description: The most common congratulatory expression in Nepali

congratulations_and_wishes.usage_context: Offering good wishes for any occasion; very versatile
congratulations_and_wishes.description: Widely used standalone or combined with occasion names (e.g., dashaĩko shubhakaamanaa)

congratulations_and_wishes.usage_context: Personal expression of good wishes, often for a specific endeavor
congratulations_and_wishes.description: Personalized form explicitly stating the wishes come from the speaker

congratulations_and_wishes.usage_context: Warm congratulations for significant achievements or celebrations
congratulations_and_wishes.description: Emphatic congratulations conveying sincere joy; common in written messages

congratulations_and_wishes.usage_context: Blessing for a new venture, journey, or undertaking
Wishing success before exams, projects, or new endeavors

congratulations_and_wishes.english: May you have a long life
congratulations_and_wishes.usage_context: Birthday blessing or general blessing for longevity; often from elders
congratulations_and_wishes.description: Traditional blessing for long life; commonly given during tika ceremonies
Common wish expressing hope for the other person's continued happiness

condolences.usage_context: Formal condolence upon hearing of a death or tragedy
condolences.description: Standard formal condolence expression used in official and personal contexts

condolences.english: May peace be found / Rest in peace
condolences.usage_context: Wishing peace for the departed soul
condolences.description: Prayer for the deceased; commonly used in condolence messages

condolences.usage_context: Religious condolence wishing divine peace for the departed
condolences.description: Common Hindu condolence prayer invoking God's peace for the soul

condolences.phrase: दिवंगत आत्माको शान्तिको लागि प्रार्थना गर्दछु
condolences.romanization: divangat aatmaako shaantiko laagi praarthanaa gardachhu
condolences.english: I pray for the peace of the departed soul
condolences.usage_context: Full formal condolence prayer, often used in written messages or speeches
condolences.description: Extended formal condolence expression; common in obituaries and official statements

condolences.usage_context: Official expression of sympathy and condolence
condolences.description: Formal condolence phrase used in official correspondence and public statements

condolences.phrase: यो दुःखद समाचार सुनेर मन भारी भयो
condolences.romanization: yo dukhad samaachaar sunera man bhaari bhayo
condolences.english: My heart is heavy hearing this sad news
condolences.usage_context: Personal expression of grief upon receiving bad news
condolences.description: More personal and emotional condolence expression

condolences.usage_context: Wishing courage and patience to the bereaved family
condolences.description: Common supportive phrase directed at the family of the deceased

condolences.usage_context: Hindu prayer for peace of the departed; used as a condolence expression
condolences.description: Sacred Hindu mantra frequently used in Nepali condolence contexts

polite_expressions.usage_context: Polite request marker; used to soften requests and instructions
polite_expressions.description: Sanskrit-derived formal please; used in polite speech and writing

polite_expressions.usage_context: Respectful acknowledgment; also used to address elders or superiors
polite_expressions.description: Multipurpose respectful word: 'yes', form of address, and attention signal

polite_expressions.usage_context: Agreement or acceptance of a request or proposal
polite_expressions.description: Very common agreement word; literally 'it happens/will be'

polite_expressions.usage_context: Expressing agreement, acceptance, or that something is satisfactory
polite_expressions.description: Universal agreement phrase; one of the most frequently used expressions
General-purpose positive adjective used as acknowledgment or praise

polite_expressions.usage_context: Politely offering a seat to a guest or visitor
polite_expressions.description: Hospitable expression; one of the first things said to guests in Nepali culture

polite_expressions.usage_context: Offering food to a guest; a key part of Nepali hospitality
polite_expressions.description: Essential hospitality phrase; refusing food is considered impolite in Nepali culture

polite_expressions.usage_context: Dismissing a concern or saying something is not necessary
polite_expressions.description: Used to reassure someone that something is not a problem

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Standard polite inquiry about someone's well-being
common_conversational_phrases.description: Common conversation starter; formal register using tapaaĩ

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Polite health inquiry; common greeting follow-up
common_conversational_phrases.description: Respectful inquiry about well-being using high honorific verb

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Expressing lack of knowledge about something
common_conversational_phrases.description: Direct statement of not knowing; universally used across registers

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Asking about a situation or event; expressing concern or curiosity
common_conversational_phrases.description: Very common question in daily conversation

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Common greeting-like question when meeting someone on the way
common_conversational_phrases.description: In Nepali culture this is a casual greeting, not necessarily expecting a detailed answer

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Common greeting showing concern for well-being; reflects food-centric hospitality
common_conversational_phrases.description: A distinctly Nepali conversational greeting; asking about meals is a way of caring

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Casual invitation to rest or chat together
common_conversational_phrases.description: Friendly conversational phrase inviting someone to spend time

common_conversational_phrases.usage_context: Asking about someone's current activity; conversation starter
common_conversational_phrases.description: Polite inquiry about current activity using honorific form

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Greeting during Dashain, Nepal's biggest festival (September-October)
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Dashain is the longest and most significant Hindu festival in Nepal, celebrating the victory of good over evil

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Greeting during Tihar, the festival of lights (October-November)
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Tihar is a five-day festival honoring various animals and Laxmi, the goddess of wealth

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Greeting for Nepali New Year (Bikram Sambat) in mid-April, or January 1
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Used for both the Bikram Sambat new year (Baisakh 1) and the Gregorian new year

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Greeting during Holi, the festival of colors (February-March)
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Holi is celebrated with colors and water; known as Fagu Purnima in Nepal

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Greeting during Chhath Parva, a sun-worshipping festival (October-November)
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Chhath is mainly celebrated in the Terai/Madhesh region of Nepal

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Greeting during Losar, the Tibetan/Tamang/Sherpa New Year (February)
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Celebrated by Tibeto-Burman communities including Tamang, Sherpa, and Gurung
Standard birthday greeting; janmadin means birthday (literally birth-day)

festival_and_seasonal_greetings.usage_context: Congratulating a couple on their wedding
festival_and_seasonal_greetings.description: Formal wedding blessing; vivaha is the Sanskrit-derived word for marriage
